trajectory_summary: The call is customer-driven from start to finish — Caitlin came in warm, led the conversation, navigated her own phone friction, and booked. Bucky's role was largely facilitative: he stayed out of the way, handled the emotional disclosures with basic decency, and got the bill. The booking is real but thin — no bill flip, no verify depth on interest, no button-up protocol elements completed. The energy in this call belongs to Caitlin, not to Bucky.
outcome_dispute: v1 outcome tag is unknown. Based on the transcript, the call ends with a booked appointment for the following morning at 10AM. Outcome should be tagged 'appointment_booked' — but the appointment is structurally fragile: no computer/no-driving anchor, no 'is that fair' handshake, format confusion (Caitlin asked if someone was 'coming over' and Bucky did not correct to phone appointment), and no personalized text tieback confirmed. Show-rate risk is elevated.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Button-up protocol completion — phone format and appointment anchors
primary_coaching_focus.quote: He'll come over, and then you guys will probably sit down, and then you'll just talk about the quote we have for you and the estimate
primary_coaching_focus.why: Bucky closed a warm, cooperative customer without completing a single required button-up element: no phone appointment specified (he described an in-person visit instead), no no-driving/computer anchor, no one-hour block stated, no 'is that fair' handshake. On this call the stakes are concrete — Caitlin is traveling Thursday, so if she no-shows Wednesday at 10AM expecting someone to show up at her ranch, the lead goes cold for two-plus weeks. The manager should walk Bucky through the button_up checklist item by item and role-play the close on this exact call.

key_moments.quote: No. No. No. No. No. I'm not all good. I want to talk to you about the solar vehicle.
key_moments.stewart_read: Caitlin is handing Bucky the single most valuable verify item on a silver platter — she's telling him exactly why she's interested. Bucky's prior line ('So you're all good, or do you need help with the design?') nearly closed the call before it started. When Caitlin corrects him, Bucky says 'Okay. Let's do it. What would you like to know?' — he inverts the dynamic entirely, putting himself in answer mode rather than anchoring her stated interest. The schema calls interest_in_solar 'THE EMOTIONAL ANCHOR' and says 'depth matters more than the answer.' Bucky never asked what drew her to solar, never reflected her stated interest back, never used it as the foundation for the pitch. Strategic read: Bucky was trying to be low-pressure. Reactive read: he missed the anchor entirely. The reactive read holds — there's no later move that picks this up.

key_moments.quote: I've already paid off the house, but, anyway, there's just some paperwork confusing right now.
key_moments.stewart_read: Paid-off house is a high-value lead signal: no mortgage means no lender approval complications, no equity concerns, and a homeowner who has demonstrated long-term financial commitment to the property. Combined with 'I'll be here for the rest of my life, probably' (05:12), this is a customer with maximum tenure alignment and zero financing friction. Bucky hears it as a title-paperwork clarification and moves on. He never acknowledges the paid-off status or the lifetime tenure signal as qualifiers that make Caitlin an exceptionally strong candidate.

key_moments.quote: My wife died about a year ago. A little over to hear that.
key_moments.stewart_read: First vulnerability disclosure. Bucky says 'Yeah. I'm sorry.' — two words — then immediately pivots to procedural reassurance about title ownership. The schema is explicit: vulnerability disclosure is a TRUST GIFT requiring acknowledgment plus a bridge to value. Bucky's response is not hostile, but it's minimal. He treats the disclosure as a paperwork explanation rather than a human moment. No bridge to how solar could help Caitlin as a solo homeowner managing a ranch on a fixed or reduced income.

key_moments.quote: everything's crazy because Deb died and Yeah. Shed pancreatic cancer. And you know what? They have a cure for it now.
key_moments.stewart_read: Second vulnerability disclosure — Caitlin returns to her wife's death unprompted, adds the detail about the cure arriving too late ('She missed it by, you know, a year and a half? That's disgusting.'). This is a more emotionally activated moment than the first. Bucky responds: 'It's terrible.' — one sentence — then silence. He does not bridge. The schema notes this is the second touch of the same pattern; the compounding effect matters. Note: 'Shed pancreatic cancer' is a Deepgram transcription artifact — likely 'She had pancreatic cancer.'
